concepts.name: leave-one-out
concepts.description:
Figures 7.14 and 7.15 examine the question of whether cross-validation does
a good job in estimating ErrT , the error conditional on a given training set
T (expression (7.15) on page 228), as opposed to the expected test error.
For each of 100 training sets generated from the “reg/linear” setting in
the top-right panel of Figure 7.3, Figure 7.14 shows the conditional error
curves ErrT as a function of subset size (top left). The next two panels show
10-fold and N-fold cross-validation, the latter also known as leave-one-out
(LOO). The thick red curve in each plot is the expected error Err, while
the thick black curves are the expected cross-validation curves. The lower
right panel shows how well cross-validation approximates the conditional
and expected error.
One might have expected N-fold CV to approximate ErrT well, since it
almost uses the full training sample to ﬁt a new test point. 10-fold CV, on
the other hand, might be expected to estimate Err well, since it averages
over somewhat diﬀerent training sets. From the ﬁgure it appears 10-fold
does a better job than N-fold in estimating ErrT , and estimates Err even
better. Indeed, the similarity of the two black curves with the red curve
suggests both CV curves are approximately unbiased for Err, with 10-fold
having less variance. Similar trends were reported by Efron (1983).
Figure 7.15 shows scatterplots of both 10-fold and N-fold cross-validation
error estimates versus the true conditional error for the 100 simulations.
Although the scatterplots do not indicate much correlation, the lower right
panel shows that for the most part the correlations are negative, a curi-
ous phenomenon that has been observed before. This negative correlation
explains why neither form of CV estimates ErrT well. The broken lines in
each plot are drawn at Err(p), the expected error for the best subset of
size p. We see again that both forms of CV are approximately unbiased for
expected error, but the variation in test error for diﬀerent training sets is
quite substantial.
Among the four experimental conditions in 7.3, this “reg/linear” scenario
showed the highest correlation between actual and predicted test error. This
concepts.existing_quote: 10-fold and N-fold cross-validation, the latter also known as leave-one-out (LOO).

paragraphs.text:
sen model by 38%, 37%, 51%, and 30%, respectively, over the four scenarios,
with BIC performing similarly. In contrast, cross-validation overestimated
the error by 1%, 4%, 0%, and 4%, with the bootstrap doing about the
same. Hence the extra work involved in computing a cross-validation or
bootstrap measure is worthwhile, if an accurate estimate of test error is
required. With other ﬁtting methods like trees, cross-validation and boot-
strap can underestimate the true error by 10%, because the search for best
tree is strongly aﬀected by the validation set. In these situations only a
separate test set will provide an unbiased estimate of test error.

Figures 7.14 and 7.15 examine the question of whether cross-validation does
a good job in estimating ErrT , the error conditional on a given training set
T (expression (7.15) on page 228), as opposed to the expected test error.

curves ErrT as a function of subset size (top left). The next two panels show

(LOO). The thick red curve in each plot is the expected error Err, while
the thick black curves are the expected cross-validation curves. The lower
right panel shows how well cross-validation approximates the conditional
and expected error.
One might have expected N-fold CV to approximate ErrT well, since it
almost uses the full training sample to ﬁt a new test point. 10-fold CV, on
the other hand, might be expected to estimate Err well, since it averages
over somewhat diﬀerent training sets. From the ﬁgure it appears 10-fold
does a better job than N-fold in estimating ErrT , and estimates Err even
better. Indeed, the similarity of the two black curves with the red curve

error estimates versus the true conditional error for the 100 simulations.
Although the scatterplots do not indicate much correlation, the lower right

ous phenomenon that has been observed before. This negative correlation
explains why neither form of CV estimates ErrT well. The broken lines in

size p. We see again that both forms of CV are approximately unbiased for
expected error, but the variation in test error for diﬀerent training sets is
quite substantial.
Among the four experimental conditions in 7.3, this “reg/linear” scenario
showed the highest correlation between actual and predicted test error. This

concepts.name: working response
concepts.description: In IRLS algorithms, an adjusted response variable computed at each iteration for fitting weighted regression.
concepts.existing_quote: This involves repeatedly fitting a weighted linear regression of a working response variable on the covariates; each regression yields a new value of the parameter estimates, which in turn give new working responses and weights.

paragraphs.text:
This is page 261
Printer: Opaque this
8
Model Inference and Averaging

For most of this book, the ﬁtting (learning) of models has been achieved by
minimizing a sum of squares for regression, or by minimizing cross-entropy
for classiﬁcation. In fact, both of these minimizations are instances of the
maximum likelihood approach to ﬁtting.
In this chapter we provide a general exposition of the maximum likeli-
hood approach, as well as the Bayesian method for inference. The boot-
strap, introduced in Chapter 7, is discussed in this context, and its relation
to maximum likelihood and Bayes is described. Finally, we present some
related techniques for model averaging and improvement, including com-
mittee methods, bagging, stacking and bumping.

The bootstrap method provides a direct computational way of assessing
uncertainty, by sampling from the training data. Here we illustrate the
bootstrap in a simple one-dimensional smoothing problem, and show its
connection to maximum likelihood.

Denote the training data by Z = {z1, z2, . . . , zN}, with zi = (xi, yi),
i = 1, 2, . . . , N. Here xi is a one-dimensional input, and yi the outcome,
either continuous or categorical. As an example, consider the N = 50 data
points shown in the left panel of Figure 8.1.
Suppose we decide to ﬁt a cubic spline to the data, with three knots
placed at the quartiles of the X values. This is a seven-dimensional lin-
ear space of functions, and can be represented, for example, by a linear
expansion of B-spline basis functions (see Section 5.9.2):

concepts.name: .632 estimator
concepts.description: A bootstrap estimate of prediction error designed to alleviate the upward bias of leave-one-out bootstrap by pulling it toward the training error rate using the constant .632.
concepts.existing_quote: The '.632 estimator' is designed to alleviate this bias. It is defined by 0.368 × err + 0.632 × 0Err(1). The derivation of the .632 estimator is complex; intuitively it pulls the leave-one out bootstrap estimate down toward the training error rate, and hence reduces its upward bias.

Denote the training data by Z = {z1, z2, . . . , zN}, with zi = (xi, yi),
i = 1, 2, . . . , N. Here xi is a one-dimensional input, and yi the outcome,

ear space of functions, and can be represented, for example, by a linear
expansion of B-spline basis functions (see Section 5.9.2):

concepts.name: .632+ estimator
concepts.description: An improved version of the .632 estimator that accounts for overfitting by adjusting the weight between leave-one-out bootstrap and training error based on the relative overfitting rate.
concepts.existing_quote: Using this, the relative overfitting rate is defined to be a quantity that ranges from 0 if there is no overfitting (0Err(1) = err) to 1 if the overfitting equals the no-information value γ̂−err. Finally, we define the '.632+' estimator by The weight w ranges from .632 if R̂ = 0 to 1 if R̂ = 1, so 0Err(.632+) ranges from 0Err(.632) to 0Err(1).

concepts.name: no-information error rate
concepts.description: The error rate of a prediction rule if the inputs and class labels were independent, used to assess the amount of overfitting in bootstrap estimates.
concepts.existing_quote: First we define γ to be the no-information error rate: this is the error rate of our prediction rule if the inputs and class labels were independent. An estimate of γ is obtained by evaluating the prediction rule on all possible combinations of targets yi and predictors xi'.

concepts.name: relative overfitting rate
concepts.description: A quantity measuring the degree of overfitting, ranging from 0 (no overfitting) to 1 (overfitting equals the no-information value), used in the .632+ estimator.
concepts.existing_quote: Using this, the relative overfitting rate is defined to be a quantity that ranges from 0 if there is no overfitting (0Err(1) = err) to 1 if the overfitting equals the no-information value γ̂−err.

concepts.name: parametric bootstrap
concepts.description: A bootstrap method that simulates new responses by adding Gaussian noise to predicted values, rather than sampling from raw data.
concepts.existing_quote: Consider a variation of the bootstrap, called the parametric bootstrap, in which we simulate new responses by adding Gaussian noise to predicted values: y∗i = μ̂(xi) + ε∗i; ε∗i ∼N(0, σ̂²); i = 1, 2, . . . , N.

uncertainty, by sampling from the training data. Here we illustrate the
bootstrap in a simple one-dimensional smoothing problem, and show its

Denote the training data by Z = {z1, z2, . . . , zN}, with zi = (xi, yi),
i = 1, 2, . . . , N. Here xi is a one-dimensional input, and yi the outcome,
either continuous or categorical. As an example, consider the N = 50 data

ear space of functions, and can be represented, for example, by a linear
expansion of B-spline basis functions (see Section 5.9.2):

concepts.name: score function
concepts.description: The derivative of the log-likelihood with respect to parameters, which equals zero at the maximum likelihood estimate.
concepts.existing_quote: The score function is defined by ℓ̇(θ; Z) = ∑ᴺᵢ₌₁ ℓ̇(θ; zi) where ℓ̇(θ; zi) = ∂ℓ(θ; zi)/∂θ. Assuming that the likelihood takes its maximum in the interior of the parameter space, ℓ̇(θ̂; Z) = 0.

This is page 261
Printer: Opaque this
8
Model Inference and Averaging

For most of this book, the ﬁtting (learning) of models has been achieved by
minimizing a sum of squares for regression, or by minimizing cross-entropy
for classiﬁcation. In fact, both of these minimizations are instances of the

hood approach, as well as the Bayesian method for inference. The boot-

related techniques for model averaging and improvement, including com-
mittee methods, bagging, stacking and bumping.

The bootstrap method provides a direct computational way of assessing
uncertainty, by sampling from the training data. Here we illustrate the
bootstrap in a simple one-dimensional smoothing problem, and show its
connection to maximum likelihood.

Denote the training data by Z = {z1, z2, . . . , zN}, with zi = (xi, yi),
i = 1, 2, . . . , N. Here xi is a one-dimensional input, and yi the outcome,

concepts.name: information matrix
concepts.description: The negative second derivative matrix of the log-likelihood, used to assess precision of maximum likelihood estimates.
concepts.existing_quote: The information matrix is I(θ) = −∂²ℓ(θ; Z)/∂θ∂θᵀ. When I(θ) is evaluated at θ = θ̂, it is often called the observed information.

concepts.description: The information matrix evaluated at the maximum likelihood estimate.

concepts.name: fisher information
concepts.description: The expected value of the information matrix, also called expected information.
concepts.existing_quote: The Fisher information (or expected information) is i(θ) = Eθ₀[I(θ)].

concepts.name: noninformative prior
concepts.description: A constant or very diffuse prior distribution that has minimal influence on the posterior, allowing data to dominate inference.
concepts.existing_quote: The distribution (8.25) with τ→∞ is called a noninformative prior for θ. In Gaussian models, maximum likelihood and parametric bootstrap analyses tend to agree with Bayesian analyses that use a noninformative prior for the free parameters.

This is page 261
Printer: Opaque this

concepts.name: gaussian process prior
concepts.description: A prior distribution on functions specified by defining the covariance between any two function values.
concepts.existing_quote: Distributions on functions are fairly complex entities: one approach is to use a Gaussian process prior in which we specify the prior covariance between any two function values μ(x) and μ(x').

This is page 261
Printer: Opaque this

concepts.name: covariance kernel
concepts.description: A function defining the covariance structure of a Gaussian process prior.
concepts.existing_quote: The implicit process prior for μ(x) is hence Gaussian, with covariance kernel K(x, x') = cov[μ(x), μ(x')] = τ·h(x)ᵀΣh(x').

placed at the quartiles of the X values. This is a seven-dimensional lin-
ear space of functions, and can be represented, for example, by a linear
expansion of B-spline basis functions (see Section 5.9.2):

concepts.name: expectation-maximization algorithm
concepts.description: An iterative algorithm for maximum likelihood estimation in problems with latent variables, alternating between computing expected values of latent data (E-step) and maximizing likelihood (M-step).
concepts.existing_quote: The EM algorithm is a popular tool for simplifying difficult maximum likelihood problems. These problems are ones for which maximization of the likelihood is difficult, but made easier by enlarging the sample with latent (unobserved) data. This is called data augmentation.

paragraphs.text:
This is page 261
Printer: Opaque this
8
Model Inference and Averaging

For most of this book, the ﬁtting (learning) of models has been achieved by
minimizing a sum of squares for regression, or by minimizing cross-entropy
for classiﬁcation. In fact, both of these minimizations are instances of the
maximum likelihood approach to ﬁtting.
In this chapter we provide a general exposition of the maximum likeli-
hood approach, as well as the Bayesian method for inference. The boot-
strap, introduced in Chapter 7, is discussed in this context, and its relation
to maximum likelihood and Bayes is described. Finally, we present some
related techniques for model averaging and improvement, including com-
mittee methods, bagging, stacking and bumping.

The bootstrap method provides a direct computational way of assessing
uncertainty, by sampling from the training data. Here we illustrate the
bootstrap in a simple one-dimensional smoothing problem, and show its
connection to maximum likelihood.

Denote the training data by Z = {z1, z2, . . . , zN}, with zi = (xi, yi),
i = 1, 2, . . . , N. Here xi is a one-dimensional input, and yi the outcome,
either continuous or categorical. As an example, consider the N = 50 data
points shown in the left panel of Figure 8.1.
Suppose we decide to ﬁt a cubic spline to the data, with three knots
placed at the quartiles of the X values. This is a seven-dimensional lin-
ear space of functions, and can be represented, for example, by a linear
expansion of B-spline basis functions (see Section 5.9.2):

concepts.name: latent data
concepts.description: Unobserved or missing data that, if known, would simplify likelihood maximization in the EM algorithm.
concepts.existing_quote: These problems are ones for which maximization of the likelihood is difficult, but made easier by enlarging the sample with latent (unobserved) data. This is called data augmentation. Here the latent data are the model memberships Δi.

paragraphs.text:
This is page 261
Printer: Opaque this
8

concepts.name: complete data log-likelihood
concepts.description: The log-likelihood based on both observed and latent data, used in the EM algorithm framework.
concepts.existing_quote: Our observed data is Z, having log-likelihood ℓ(θ; Z) depending on parameters θ. The latent or missing data is Zm, so that the complete data is T = (Z, Zm) with log-likelihood ℓ0(θ; T), ℓ0 based on the complete density.

This is page 261
Printer: Opaque this
8

expansion of B-spline basis functions (see Section 5.9.2):

concepts.name: markov chain monte carlo
concepts.description: A class of algorithms for sampling from probability distributions by constructing a Markov chain with the desired distribution as its stationary distribution.
concepts.existing_quote: Having defined a Bayesian model, one would like to draw samples from the resulting posterior distribution, in order to make inferences about the parameters. In this section we discuss the Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) approach to posterior sampling.

This is page 261
Printer: Opaque this
8

concepts.name: gibbs sampling
concepts.description: An MCMC method that generates samples from a joint distribution by iteratively sampling from conditional distributions of each variable given the others.
concepts.existing_quote: The Gibbs sampling procedure alternatively simulates from each of these distributions and when the process stabilizes, provides a sample from the desired joint distribution. Under regularity conditions it can be shown that this procedure eventually stabilizes, and the resulting random variables are indeed a sample from the joint distribution.

This is page 261
Printer: Opaque this
8

concepts.name: stationary distribution
concepts.description: In MCMC, the limiting distribution of the Markov chain, which is the target distribution from which we wish to sample.
concepts.existing_quote: More formally, Gibbs sampling produces a Markov chain whose stationary distribution is the true joint distribution, and hence the term 'Markov chain Monte Carlo.'
